---
title: "CBSE / NCERT Track — Class 8 to Class 12"
subtitle: "A board-exam-aligned path through Indian school mathematics, chapter by chapter"
---

## Why this track exists

The seven tiers of *Applied Maths* synthesise the best of Pestalozzi,
the Russian problem-first school, the Socratic tradition, and the
Bhāskara sūtra style — they are the curriculum any learner would walk if
they had no exam to sit and all the time in the world.

The **CBSE / NCERT track** is for learners who have an exam to sit, on a
specific date, on a specific syllabus. It is aligned, chapter by chapter,
to the rationalised **NCERT 2023 syllabus** for Classes 8 through 12,
and every lesson includes a **Board-Pattern Practice** block with the
question styles you will see in the actual board paper.

Use this track if any of the following describe you:

- You are sitting the **CBSE Class 10** or **CBSE Class 12** board exam.
- You are studying CBSE in **Class 8, 9, or 11** and want lessons that
  follow your school textbook.
- You are preparing for **JEE / NEET** and want a clean conceptual
  foundation that starts from the same syllabus you have already begun.

## How this track is organised

```
Class 8 → Class 9 → Class 10 (Board) → Class 11 → Class 12 (Board)
```

Each class is a **separate tier** in the curriculum manifest. Within a
class, the lesson numbering matches the **NCERT chapter number** —
Lesson 5 in Class 10 is Arithmetic Progressions because that is NCERT
Class 10 Chapter 5. Where a chapter contains two distinct ideas
("one idea per lesson" — see [CLAUDE.md](https://github.com/rajeshpillai/applied-maths/blob/main/CLAUDE.md)),
it is split with an `a/b/c` suffix (e.g. `08a-trigonometry-intro`,
`08b-trigonometric-identities`).

## How a CBSE lesson is built

Every CBSE lesson follows the same six-beat contract as the synthesis
curriculum (**Touch → Wonder → Discover → Name → Apply → Connect**) and
adds two CBSE-specific extras:

1. **Apply → Board-Pattern Practice.** After the Warm / Working / Stretch
   sets, every lesson has a block with **1-mark, 2-mark, 3-mark, and
   4-or-5-mark style** problems, each with full graduated hints and a
   worked solution. We also point you to the relevant **NCERT Exemplar**
   problem and at least one **previous-year question** where applicable.

2. **Connect → NCERT chapter alignment.** Every lesson tells you exactly
   which NCERT chapter and section it covers, plus a link to the
   **sister synthesis lesson** in the main tiers (different pedagogical
   angle on the same idea — useful when one explanation does not land).

Everything else — the pre-flight check, multiple representations,
graduated hints, worked solutions, common confusions, self-assessment —
is identical to the rest of the curriculum.

## The five classes

- **[Class 8](../lessons/tier-cbse-08-class-8/index.qmd)** — 13 chapters.
  Rational numbers, linear equations, quadrilaterals, mensuration,
  exponents, factorisation, graphs.
- **[Class 9](../lessons/tier-cbse-09-class-9/index.qmd)** — 12 chapters.
  Number systems, polynomials, coordinate geometry, Euclid's geometry,
  triangles, circles, Heron's formula, surface areas & volumes,
  statistics.
- **[Class 10 (Board)](../lessons/tier-cbse-10-class-10/index.qmd)** —
  14 chapters. The first board year. Real numbers, polynomials,
  quadratics, arithmetic progressions, similarity, coordinate geometry,
  trigonometry, circles, mensuration, statistics, probability.
- **[Class 11](../lessons/tier-cbse-11-class-11/index.qmd)** —
  14 chapters. Sets, relations and functions, trigonometry deepened,
  complex numbers, permutations & combinations, binomial theorem,
  sequences & series, straight lines, conic sections, 3D geometry,
  limits, derivatives, statistics, probability.
- **[Class 12 (Board)](../lessons/tier-cbse-12-class-12/index.qmd)** —
  13 chapters. The final board year. Inverse trig, matrices,
  determinants, calculus (continuity, differentiability, integrals,
  applications), differential equations, vectors, 3D geometry, linear
  programming, probability.

## How to use this track

1. **Walk it in order, one chapter at a time.** Start with the first
   lesson of your class. Do not skip ahead — the prerequisite chain
   matches NCERT's own.
2. **Tick off the self-assessment before moving on.** If two of three
   answers are *no*, redo the Stretch and Board-Pattern problems before
   the next lesson.
3. **Use the synthesis lessons when something doesn't click.** The
   "sister synthesis lesson" link in each CBSE lesson's *Connect* beat
   leads to a different pedagogical treatment of the same idea — often
   the second angle is what makes it land.
4. **Treat the Board-Pattern Practice as the minimum, not the ceiling.**
   The problems mirror board styles. For higher-stakes preparation
   (Olympiad, JEE Advanced, NEET), pair each lesson with the
   corresponding tier-N synthesis lesson's Stretch problems.

## Honouring NCERT, not replacing it

This curriculum supplements your NCERT textbook — it does not replace
it. Every lesson points you back to the relevant NCERT sections so you
read the canonical exposition alongside this one. The board exam is
written from NCERT; treat that book as primary and this site as the
*"explain this to me again, with widgets"* layer.

::: {.callout-note}
The lesson statuses on this page reflect a snapshot of `_curriculum.yml`
at build time. Lessons marked **planned** are scheduled but may not yet
have a written page; lessons marked **done** are complete.
:::
